problem.title: Palindrome Linked List
problem.description: Given the head of a singly linked list, return true if it is a palindrome or false otherwise.
assumptions: Return true if linked list reads the same forward and backward.

example.explanation: The list reads the same forwards and backwards.

solutions.title: Solution 1 – Reverse Second Half (Optimal)
solutions.description: Find the middle of the list using slow/fast pointers, reverse the second half in-place, then compare node by node with the first half. The key insight is that reversing only the second half avoids extra space while enabling a direct comparison.
solutions.code_python:
# class ListNode:
#     def __init__(self, val=0, next=None):
#         self.val = val
#         self.next = next

from typing import Optional

def isPalindrome(head: Optional[object]) -> bool:
    if not head or not head.next:
        return True

    # Find the middle using slow/fast pointers
    slow, fast = head, head
    while fast.next and fast.next.next:
        slow = slow.next
        fast = fast.next.next

    # Reverse the second half
    second_half = reverse_list(slow.next)
    first_half = head

    # Compare the two halves
    runner = second_half
    while runner:
        if first_half.val != runner.val:
            return False
        first_half = first_half.next
        runner = runner.next

    return True

def reverse_list(head: Optional[object]) -> Optional[object]:
    prev = None
    current = head
    while current:
        next_temp = current.next
        current.next = prev
        prev = current
        current = next_temp
    return prev

solutions.title: Solution 2 – Stack Approach
solutions.description: Use a stack to store the first half and compare with the second half.
solutions.code_python:
from typing import Optional

def isPalindrome_stack(head: Optional[object]) -> bool:
    if not head or not head.next:
        return True

    # Find the middle
    slow, fast = head, head
    while fast.next and fast.next.next:
        slow = slow.next
        fast = fast.next.next

    # Push first half onto stack
    stack = []
    current = head
    while current is not slow.next:
        stack.append(current.val)
        current = current.next

    # For odd-length lists, skip the middle element
    if not fast.next:
        stack.pop()

    # Compare second half with stack
    while current:
        if stack.pop() != current.val:
            return False
        current = current.next

    return True
